data.title: Diana’s Higher Bid Puts Genco Valuation And Control Premium In Focus
data.description:

data.title: Is La-Z-Boy (LZB) Now Pricing In Too Much Pessimism After Recent Share Weakness
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether La-Z-Boy is quietly becoming good value or just a value trap, starting with a clear look at its current pricing can help set expectations before you make any move.
  • The share price sits at US$34.57, with returns of a 3.2% decline over 7 days, a 10.6% decline over 30 days, a 7.3% decline year to date and a 15.0% decline over 1 year, while the 3 year return stands at 25.8% and the 5 year return at a 15.0% decline.
  • Recent headlines around La-Z-Boy have focused on its position in the consumer durables space and how demand for home furnishings is shaping investor sentiment. This backdrop helps explain why short term price moves have looked quite different to the longer term return profile.
  • Our valuation checks give La-Z-Boy a score of 3 out of 6, which suggests parts of the business may be priced more attractively than others. Next we will walk through the key valuation approaches before finishing with a broader way to judge whether that score really captures the full picture.

Find out why La-Z-Boy's -15.0% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: La-Z-Boy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows, then discounts them back to today’s dollars to arrive at an estimated “fair value” per share.

For La-Z-Boy, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s last twelve months free cash flow sits at about $171.2 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, with projected free cash flow of $126.8 million in 2029, and Simply Wall St extends this out using its own assumptions through 2035.

These annual projections, all in $, are discounted back to the present and summed to estimate what the entire stream of future cash flows could be worth today based on these assumptions. On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $51.05 per share.

With the current share price at $34.57, the model implies the stock trades at roughly a 32.3% discount to this intrinsic value, which suggests La-Z-Boy appears undervalued on this cash flow view, given the model’s inputs and assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED (based on this DCF model)

Approach 2: La-Z-Boy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like La-Z-Boy, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many years of today's earnings are reflected in the current share price.

What counts as a "normal" P/E partly comes down to what the market expects for future earnings growth and how risky those earnings look. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower multiple.

La-Z-Boy currently trades on a P/E of 16.91x. That compares with an average of 12.00x for the wider Consumer Durables industry and a peer group average of 12.20x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary "Fair Ratio" of 18.65x, which is the P/E level suggested by factors such as La-Z-Boy’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and key risks. This Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific inputs rather than treating all firms as alike. Since the current P/E of 16.91x is below the Fair Ratio of 18.65x, La-Z-Boy screens as trading at a discount on this measure.

data.title: RadNet Expands Global AI Role With Gleamer Deal And DeepHealth Platform
data.description:
• RadNet acquired Gleamer, expanding its radiology clinical AI footprint across new disease areas and geographies.
  • At ECR 2026, its DeepHealth unit presented a broad clinical AI platform aimed at integrating multiple imaging solutions.
  • Together, these moves signal a shift toward a more global, AI driven digital health offering for RadNet.

For investors watching NasdaqGM:RDNT, the timing is important. The company’s share price stands at $60.68, with a value score of 1 and a 1 year return of 20.6%, alongside very large multi year gains over 3 and 5 years. In that context, a move into a wider, AI focused radiology offering could be material for how the business is viewed.

Gleamer’s tools and the expanded DeepHealth platform suggest a broader role for RadNet in clinical AI, not just imaging centers. These steps may influence how the company is positioned in global radiology workflows, patient access, and digital health services, which are key themes many investors are watching closely.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for RadNet by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on RadNet.

2 things going right for RadNet that this headline doesn't cover.

The Gleamer acquisition and the DeepHealth platform reveal how RadNet is trying to move from being mainly an imaging-center operator to a broader clinical AI and digital health provider. Gleamer brings FDA-cleared and CE-marked tools that are already used in 44 countries across more than 25 indications, while DeepHealth is pitching a single platform that supports multiple imaging modalities for both screening and acute care. That combination can make RadNet more comparable to radiology AI players like GE HealthCare or Siemens Healthineers rather than only outpatient peers. At the same time, the full year 2025 results show revenue of US$2.04b but a net loss of US$18.65m, so investors may want to weigh the cost of this push into AI, including up to €230m for Gleamer and the absorption of 130 employees, against management’s focus on productivity gains and recurring software revenue.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The RadNet Narrative

  • The move deepens RadNet’s AI-powered workflow and imaging efficiency push, directly tying into the narrative that higher-margin procedures and automation can support operating margin expansion.
  • The higher spend on digital health and acquisitions, on top of a full year net loss, ties back to concerns that heavy AI and M&A investment could strain free cash flow and make execution on 2025 to 2028 targets harder.
  • The global reach of Gleamer and DeepHealth’s unified platform, especially in Europe, adds an international digital health angle that is not fully reflected in a story focused mainly on U.S. outpatient imaging growth and payer arrangements.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for RadNet to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integrating Gleamer into DeepHealth, while also rolling out a broad AI platform, adds execution risk on technology, staff and customer migration, especially as RadNet already manages multiple prior AI acquisitions.
  • ⚠️ The company reported a net loss of US$18.65m for 2025 and is committing up to €230m for Gleamer, so the payback period on these AI investments and their effect on cash generation is a key open question.
  • 🎁 DeepHealth’s platform and Gleamer’s 25 plus indication portfolio could support higher imaging throughput and more routine AI usage, which management has linked to operational efficiency and higher-margin procedures.
  • 🎁 The Gleamer deal expands RadNet’s reach outside the U.S., giving it a broader installed base and data footprint that may help it compete with larger imaging and AI vendors such as GE HealthCare and Philips.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track how quickly Gleamer’s tools are integrated into DeepHealth’s single platform and whether management starts to quantify productivity gains in areas like X-ray reading volumes and reporting times. The dedicated March 5 call on digital health and the Gleamer deal should also be a reference point for any new disclosures on recurring AI revenue, customer churn, and expected operating costs. Keep an eye on how Digital Health revenue trends against the 47.6% year on year growth reported in Q4 2025, how Imaging Center margins hold up alongside higher technology spend, and whether RadNet’s AI offering wins new contracts or partnerships that put it more directly up against larger radiology AI and imaging providers.

data.description:

Margins Slip To 2.4% On Lower Profit

  • Over the last twelve months, Piaggio & C generated about €1.5b of revenue and €36.7 million of net income, which equates to a 2.4% net margin compared with 3.9% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bearish narrative is that this thinner 2.4% margin sits alongside a modest 3.3% average yearly earnings growth over five years. Critics link this to pressures such as high logistics costs and tougher competition, even though recent quarters like Q2 FY 2025 still showed net income of €21.4 million on €481.9 million of revenue.
    • Bears argue that the drop from 3.9% to 2.4% margin fits with concerns about rising costs and market share pressure in core regions.
    • At the same time, the fact that Piaggio & C remained profitable every quarter in the period, with net income between about €1.5 million and €33.4 million, shows earnings are under pressure but not collapsing, which challenges the harshest bearish claims.

Earnings Forecasts Outrun 2.4% Revenue Growth

  • Supporters of the bullish view see this gap between faster earnings growth and slower top line growth as a sign that margin expansion could become a key earnings driver. This lines up with expectations that profit margins rise from about 2.9% to 6.4% and that earnings reach €123.6 million by around 2028.
    • Backers of this bullish case point to the recent record gross margin and EBITDA mentioned in management commentary, arguing that the current 2.4% net margin does not fully reflect operational improvements already in place.

data.title: Assessing Monte Paschi (BIT:BMPS) After Sharp Share Price Pullback And Turnaround Focus
data.description:

Approach 1: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a bank creates over and above the return that shareholders require. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it starts from the equity on the balance sheet and asks whether the forecast return on that equity justifies the current share price.

For Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, book value is €19.98 per share and the stable book value used in the model is €8.99 per share, based on future book value estimates from 6 analysts. Stable EPS is €0.88 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 10 analysts, while the average return on equity used is 9.78%.

The model applies a cost of equity of €0.90 per share. That leads to an excess return of a €0.02 per share shortfall, which is then capitalised to arrive at an intrinsic value estimate of €8.63 per share. Compared with the current share price, this Excess Returns valuation implies the stock trades at a 17.0% discount, so it screens as undervalued on this approach.

Approach 2: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful, quick way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how many years of current earnings the market is effectively pricing in.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on what investors expect for future growth and how risky they see those earnings. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often go with a higher P/E, while lower expected growth or higher perceived risk often goes with a lower P/E.

Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena currently trades on a P/E of 8.02x. That compares with an Italian Banks industry average of about 10.87x and a peer group average of 12.19x, so the stock is on a lower multiple than both of these simple benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is an estimate of what a company’s P/E “should” be, based on factors like its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. That makes it a more tailored yardstick than a basic comparison with peers or industry averages, which treat very different businesses as if they were the same.

For Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, the Fair Ratio is 11.09x versus the current 8.02x. On this preferred multiple, the shares currently appear cheaper relative to that Fair Ratio.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Is Walt Disney (DIS) Fairly Priced After Recent Share Price Weakness And Media Sector Reassessment
data.description:

  • The stock last closed at US$101.54, and recent returns show a 4.2% fall over the past week, a 5.1% decline over the last month, and a 9.2% decline year to date, with a 2.7% decline over the past year and an 11.1% gain over three years, while the five year return sits at a 47.3% decline.
  • Recent news coverage has focused on how investors are reassessing large media and entertainment groups as they balance traditional TV, parks, and streaming. For Disney, that conversation has kept attention on how its mix of assets is being priced, which helps frame why the share price has moved in the way it has.

Find out why Walt Disney's -2.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Walt Disney Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of future cash a business could generate and discounts those back into today’s dollars to arrive at an implied value per share.

For Walt Disney, the model uses last twelve months Free Cash Flow of about $8.65b as a starting point, then projects future Free Cash Flow in dollars using analyst estimates for the next few years and extrapolated numbers after that. For example, Simply Wall St’s projections include Free Cash Flow of $10.18b in 2026 and $11.24b in 2028, extending out to $15.06b by 2035.

Discounting this stream of projected cash flows back to today using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $95.72 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $101.54, this DCF output suggests the stock is around 6.1% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap and within a range many investors might see as broadly in line with fundamentals.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Walt Disney is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Walt Disney.

Approach 2: Walt Disney Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Walt Disney, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support that price. Investors usually look for a P/E that lines up with their expectations for future growth and the level of risk they are taking on, with higher growth or lower perceived risk often justifying a higher "normal" P/E ratio.

Disney currently trades on a P/E of about 14.7x. That sits below the Entertainment industry average of around 35.2x and also below the peer group average of about 48.0x. On the surface, that gap indicates the market is valuing Disney’s earnings at a lower multiple than many other companies in its space.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Disney is 25.3x. Because it adjusts for these company specific traits, the Fair Ratio can provide a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages. Compared with the current P/E of 14.7x, the Fair Ratio shows the shares are trading below that modeled level.
